items.title: Identifying Nephrologic Syndromes through Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Nephrologic syndromes are identified by a combination of clinical symptoms, physical findings, and laboratory changes. These include reductions in GFR, abnormalities in urine sediment, proteinuria, disturbances in urine volume, hypertension/edema, electrolyte imbalances, and sometimes fever/pain.
keyPoints: Reduction in glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
Abnormalities of urine sediment (RBCs, WBCs, casts, crystals)
Proteinuria
Disturbances in urine volume (oliguria, anuria, polyuria)
Hypertension and/or edema
Electrolyte abnormalities
Fever/pain in some syndromes
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.title: Critical Aspects for Distinguishing Renal Syndromes
items.content: To distinguish among renal processes, focus on reduction in GFR, alterations of urinary sediment/protein excretion, and abnormalities of urinary volume. These aspects are crucial for narrowing differential diagnoses and determining appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.
keyPoints: Reduction in glomerular filtration rate (GFR)
Alterations of the urinary sediment and/or protein excretion
Abnormalities of urinary volume
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.title: Why Monitoring GFR is Crucial in Clinical Settings
items.content: Monitoring the Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) is essential for assessing kidney function. Direct measurement involves using radioactive isotopes like inulin or iothalamate, but this method is impractical in most clinical settings. Instead, plasma creatinine levels are used as a surrogate to estimate GFR.
keyPoints: Direct GFR measurement uses isotopes such as inulin or iothalamate.
Plasma creatinine ($P_{cr}$) serves as a common surrogate for estimating GFR.
Failure to adjust drug dosing based on reduced GFR can lead to significant morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.title: How Creatinine Clearance Estimates GFR
items.content: Creatinine clearance (CrCl) is an approximation of GFR, calculated using plasma and urinary creatinine excretion rates. It provides a practical method to estimate kidney function when direct measurement isn't feasible.
keyPoints: CrCl formula: CrCl = (U_vol × U_Cr)/(P_Cr × T_min).
For 20- to 50-year-old men, creatinine excretion should be 18.5–25.0 mg/kg body weight; for women, it's 16.5–22.4 mg/kg.
An 80-kg man should excrete between ~1500 and 2000 mg of creatinine in an adequate collection.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.title: When to Use Cockcroft-Gault and MDRD Formulas for Estimating Kidney Function
items.content: In the absence of timed creatinine clearance collections, formulas like Cockcroft-Gault and four-variable MDRD are used to estimate kidney function from plasma creatinine levels.
keyPoints: Cockcroft-Gault formula: CrCl(mL/min)={{(140-age)x Lean Body Weight(kg)}{Serum Creatinine(mg/dL)x72}}(x0.85 if female).
MDRD formula: eGFR (mL/min per 1.73 m^2)=186.3 x $P_{Cr}$($e^{-1.154}$) x age($e^{-0.203}$) x (0.742 if female) x (1.21 if black).
CKD-EPI formula is considered more accurate and accounts for sex, race, and plasma creatinine levels.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.title: Limitations of Creatinine-Based GFR Estimation
items.content: Creatinine-based methods for estimating glomerular filtration rate (GFR) have inherent limitations, particularly when the patient is not in a steady state or has conditions affecting muscle mass. The MDRD equation correlates better with true GFR at values below 60 mL/min per 1.73 m². Variations in muscle mass due to chronic illness, glucocorticoid use, or malnutrition can obscure significant changes in GFR.
keyPoints: Creatinine-based estimates assume steady state without daily fluctuations in plasma creatinine (P_{Cr}).
MDRD equation is more accurate for GFR < 60 mL/min per 1.73 m².
Muscle mass loss can mask changes in GFR with minimal P_{Cr} variations.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.title: Cystatin C as an Adjunct to Creatinine-Based eGFR
items.content: Cystatin C is a more sensitive marker for early GFR decline compared to plasma creatinine, with less influence from muscle mass. However, it is affected by sex, diabetes mellitus, smoking, and inflammation. Combining creatinine and cystatin C in eGFR equations enhances accuracy. Clinical judgment remains crucial, especially in cases like bodybuilders where creatinine-based estimates may underestimate GFR.
keyPoints: Cystatin C levels are less affected by muscle mass compared to plasma creatinine.
Influenced by sex, diabetes mellitus, smoking, and inflammation.
Equations combining creatinine and cystatin C provide more accurate eGFR estimates.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Prerenal Azotemia: Causes and Mechanisms
items.content: Prerenal azotemia is primarily due to decreased renal perfusion, accounting for 40-80% of acute renal failure cases. It results from conditions that reduce circulating blood volume or effective arterial volume, such as gastrointestinal hemorrhage, burns, sepsis, and severe heart failure. The body's compensatory mechanisms include activation of the sympathetic nervous system, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, and vasopressin release to maintain glomerular filtration rate (GFR). However, GFR declines steeply if mean arterial pressure falls below 80 mmHg.
keyPoints: Prerenal azotemia accounts for 40–80% of acute renal failure cases.
Causes include decreased circulating blood volume or effective arterial volume.
Compensatory mechanisms: sympathetic nervous system, renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, and vasopressin release.
GFR declines steeply if mean arterial pressure falls below 80 mmHg.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.title: Distinguishing Prerenal Azotemia from Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN)
items.content: Prerenal azotemia can be differentiated from ATN using urinalysis and urinary electrolyte measurements. In prerenal conditions, the urine is concentrated (>500 mosmol), with low sodium concentration (<20 mmol/L) and fractional excretion of Na (FE_Na) <1%. The FE_Na is typically >1% in ATN but may be <1% in milder cases or underlying prerenal disorders. Urine sediment in prerenal azotemia is usually normal or shows hyaline and granular casts, whereas ATN presents with cellular debris and dark granular casts.
keyPoints: Prerenal urine: concentrated (>500 mosmol), low Na (<20 mmol/L), FE_Na <1%.
ATN urine: FE_Na typically >1%, may be <1% in milder cases or prerenal disorders.
Prerenal sediment: normal or hyaline/granular casts.
ATN sediment: cellular debris, tubular epithelial casts, dark granular casts.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.title: Risks of NSAIDs and ACE Inhibitors/ARBs in Prerenal Azotemia
items.content: Patients taking NSAIDs or ACE inhibitors/ARBs are at higher risk for hemodynamically mediated acute renal failure, especially when blood volume or arterial perfusion pressure is reduced. NSAIDs can cause severe vasoconstriction by blocking prostaglandin production, while ACE inhibitors/ARBs decrease efferent arteriolar tone and glomerular capillary perfusion pressure. Patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis are particularly susceptible to a decline in GFR when given these medications.
keyPoints: NSAIDs block prostaglandin production, causing vasoconstriction.
ACE inhibitors/ARBs decrease efferent arteriolar tone and perfusion pressure.
Increased risk of acute renal failure with reduced blood volume or arterial pressure.
Patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis are particularly susceptible.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.title: Diagnosing Postrenal Azotemia
items.content: Postrenal azotemia, while accounting for less than 5% of acute renal failure cases, is often reversible and should be ruled out early. It requires obstruction at critical points such as the urethra or bladder outlet, bilateral ureteral obstruction, or unilateral obstruction in a single functioning kidney.
keyPoints: Postrenal azotemia accounts for <5% of acute renal failure cases.
Complete obstructive acute renal failure requires specific types of obstructions.
Diagnosis often involves renal ultrasound; however, early stages may not show dilation.
Alternative imaging like furosemide renogram (MAG3 nuclear medicine study) can be necessary.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Intrinsic Renal Disease
items.content: Intrinsic renal disease is diagnosed when prerenal and postrenal azotemia are excluded. It involves processes affecting large vessels, microvasculature, glomeruli, or the tubulointerstitium, with ischemic and toxic acute tubular necrosis (ATN) accounting for ~90% of cases.
keyPoints: Intrinsic renal disease is diagnosed after excluding prerenal and postrenal causes.
~90% of acute intrinsic renal failure cases are due to ischemic and toxic ATN.
Ischemic ATN often follows major surgery, trauma, or severe hypovolemia.
Nephrotoxic ATN can result from medications causing vasoconstriction, direct toxicity, or tubular obstruction.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Features of Acute Tubular Necrosis
items.content: Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) can be distinguished from prerenal azotemia by urinalysis and urine electrolyte composition. Ischemic ATN is common in patients with major surgery or trauma, while nephrotoxic ATN results from medications that cause renal vasoconstriction or direct toxicity.
keyPoints: ATN can be distinguished from prerenal azotemia by urinalysis and urine electrolyte composition.
Ischemic ATN is common after major surgery, trauma, severe hypovolemia, sepsis, or burns.
Nephrotoxic ATN results from medications causing vasoconstriction, direct tubule toxicity, or obstruction.
Discontinuation of nephrotoxins and blood pressure stabilization often suffice without dialysis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.title: Significance of RBC Casts in Urine for Glomerular Disease Diagnosis
items.content: The presence of red blood cell (RBC) casts in urine is a critical indicator for early renal biopsy due to its implications on diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment. While highly specific for glomerulonephritis, RBC casts are insensitive, meaning hematuria without RBC casts can still suggest glomerular disease. Enhanced specificity of urine microscopy can be achieved using phase contrast microscopes to detect dysmorphic red cells (acanthocytes).
keyPoints: RBC casts in urine indicate the need for early renal biopsy.
Highly specific but insensitive marker for glomerulonephritis.
Hematuria without RBC casts can still suggest glomerular disease.
Phase contrast microscopy enhances specificity by detecting dysmorphic red cells (acanthocytes).
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.title: Clinical Implications of Oliguria and Anuria in Renal Function Assessment
items.content: Oliguria is defined as a 24-hour urine output less than 400 mL, while anuria refers to the complete absence of urine formation (<100 mL). Causes of anuria include bilateral urinary tract obstruction, vascular catastrophes, renal vein thrombosis, acute cast nephropathy in myeloma, and severe conditions like ATN or rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Oliguria is abnormal as it indicates insufficient production to excrete the daily osmolar load. Nonoliguric acute tubular necrosis (ATN) presents with less severe disturbances of potassium and hydrogen balance and typically has a more rapid recovery.
keyPoints: Oliguria: 24-h urine output <400 mL.
Anuria: Complete absence of urine formation (<100 mL).
Causes of anuria include bilateral urinary tract obstruction, vascular catastrophes, renal vein thrombosis, acute cast nephropathy in myeloma, severe ATN, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.
Oliguria indicates insufficient production to excrete the daily osmolar load.
Nonoliguric ATN has less severe electrolyte disturbances and faster recovery.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.title: Understanding Dipstick Limitations in Proteinuria Detection
items.content: Dipstick tests for proteinuria primarily detect albumin and can yield false positives under certain conditions such as urine pH >7.0, high concentration, or contamination with blood. They may also miss significant non-albumin proteinuria.
keyPoints: Dipsticks are limited to detecting urinary albumin.
False-positive results occur at pH >7.0 or when urine is very concentrated or contaminated with blood.
Non-albumin proteins, such as Bence-Jones proteins in multiple myeloma, may be missed by dipstick screening.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.title: Quantifying Proteinuria: Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio
items.content: The albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) in a spot urine sample approximates the 24-hour albumin excretion rate (AER), aiding in assessing proteinuria levels and potential renal injury.
keyPoints: ACR (mg/g) ≈ AER (mg/24 h).
Healthy individuals typically excrete <150 mg/d of total protein and <30 mg/d of albumin.
Even low-level albuminuria (<30 mg/d) increases risk for nephropathy or cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms and Implications of Proteinuria
items.content: Proteinuria results from disrupted glomerular barriers, leading to leakage of proteins into urine. It can indicate various renal diseases, including nephrotic syndrome when protein excretion exceeds 3.5 g/day.
keyPoints: Normal glomeruli prevent albumin and large proteins (>100 kDa) from passing through.
Disruption in glomerular structure leads to selective or nonselective proteinuria.
>3.5 g/day of urinary protein excretion often indicates nephrotic syndrome, but can occur without its full features.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.title: Mechanisms and Compensations in Nephrotic Syndrome
items.content: In nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminemia results from excessive urinary albumin loss and increased catabolism by the proximal tubule. Edema arises due to renal sodium retention and decreased plasma oncotic pressure, prompting fluid shift into interstitium. The body compensates through activation of the renin-angiotensin system, AVP stimulation, and sympathetic nervous system activation, leading to further salt and water reabsorption.
keyPoints: Hypoalbuminemia results from urinary losses and increased proximal tubule catabolism.
Edema is due to renal sodium retention and reduced plasma oncotic pressure.
Compensatory mechanisms include renin-angiotensin system activation, AVP stimulation, and sympathetic nervous system activation.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Hematuria
items.content: Isolated hematuria without proteinuria or casts often indicates urinary tract bleeding. Microscopic hematuria is defined as 2-5 RBCs per HPF and can be detected by dipstick. Persistent or significant hematuria (>3 RBCs/HPF on three urinalyses, >100 RBCs in a single urinalysis, or gross hematuria) suggests renal or urologic lesions in 9.1% of cases.
keyPoints: Isolated hematuria often indicates urinary tract bleeding.
Microscopic hematuria: 2-5 RBCs/HPF; detectable by dipstick.
Persistent/significant hematuria (>3 RBCs/HPF on three urinalyses, >100 RBCs/single urinalysis, or gross hematuria) is associated with renal/urologic lesions in 9.1% of cases.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.title: Glomerular Hematuria and Its Implications
items.content: Isolated microscopic hematuria can indicate glomerular disease, with dysmorphic RBCs suggesting a glomerular origin. Common causes include IgA nephropathy, hereditary nephritis, and thin basement membrane disease. Hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, and protein excretion >500 mg/d is virtually diagnostic of glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: Isolated microscopic hematuria can indicate glomerular disease.
Dysmorphic RBCs suggest a glomerular origin.
Hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts, and protein excretion >500 mg/d is diagnostic of glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.title: Distinguishing Water Diuresis from Solute Diuresis in Polyuria
items.content: Polyuria is characterized by urine output >3 L/d. To differentiate between water diuresis and solute diuresis, measure urine osmolality. A water diuresis presents with dilute urine (<250 mosmol/L) despite high volume, indicating issues like polydipsia or diabetes insipidus. Solute diuresis is indicated by urine osmolality >300 mosmol/L, necessitating identification of the solute causing increased excretion.
keyPoints: Polyuria: urine output >3 L/d
Water diuresis: dilute urine (<250 mosmol/L), normal total osmolar excretion
Solute diuresis: urine osmolality >300 mosmol/L, requires identification of solutes
Common causes of water diuresis include polydipsia and diabetes insipidus
Common cause of solute diuresis is poorly controlled diabetes mellitus with glucosuria
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.title: Causes and Consequences of Solute Diuresis
items.content: Solute diuresis occurs when excessive filtration of a poorly reabsorbed solute, such as glucose or mannitol, leads to increased urine output. This results in volume depletion and serum hypertonicity due to disproportionate water loss relative to sodium. Hypernatremia can occur because the urine sodium concentration is lower than that of blood.
keyPoints: Solute diuresis: excessive filtration of poorly reabsorbed solutes like glucose or mannitol
Consequences include volume depletion and serum hypertonicity
Hypernatremia occurs due to disproportionate water loss compared to sodium
Common iatrogenic causes: mannitol, radiocontrast media, high-protein feedings
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in nephrologic syndromes?
options: Abnormal urinary excretion of serum proteins
items.explanation: Increased heart rate is not typically included as an element of nephrologic syndromes.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation that may accompany some renal syndromes?
items.explanation: Fever/pain can be a clinical manifestation in some renal syndromes.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.question: Which of the following is an abnormality that may arise from systemic illness or primary renal disease?
items.explanation: All listed abnormalities may arise from systemic illness or primary renal disease.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.question: Which of the following substances is directly filtered at the glomerulus and neither reabsorbed nor secreted throughout the tubule for measuring GFR?
items.explanation: Inulin is used as a marker for direct measurement of GFR because it is filtered at the glomerulus and not reabsorbed or secreted.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary surrogate marker used to estimate GFR in most clinical settings?
items.explanation: Plasma creatinine level is widely used as a surrogate to estimate GFR.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.question: Which formula for estimating GFR takes into account age, sex, and race?
items.explanation: The CKD-EPI formula considers age, sex, and race in estimating GFR.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a limitation of using creatinine clearance to estimate GFR?
options: Creatinine is not produced from muscle metabolism.
Creatinine is reabsorbed by the tubules.
Creatinine can be secreted into the proximal tubule, especially in advanced CKD.
Creatinine levels are unaffected by dietary intake.
items.explanation: In advanced CKD, creatinine can be secreted into the proximal tubule, leading to an overestimation of GFR.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.question: Which equation is better correlated with true GFR when the GFR is less than 60 mL/min per 1.73 m²?
items.explanation: The MDRD equation is better correlated with true GFR when the GFR is <60 mL/min per 1.73 m².
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.question: What factor has led to some medical centers stopping the use of race modifiers in eGFR calculations?
options: Introduction of cystatin C as a marker
items.explanation: Race is considered a social rather than biological construct, leading to scrutiny and changes in the use of race modifiers.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.question: Which marker has been proposed as more sensitive for early GFR decline due to lesser effects of muscle mass?
items.explanation: Cystatin C is considered a more sensitive marker for early GFR decline with lesser effects from muscle mass.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is typically seen in patients with prerenal azotemia?
options: Dark granular casts in urine sediment
items.explanation: In prerenal azotemia, the tubules are intact leading to avid sodium retention with a urine Na concentration typically less than 20 mmol/L.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.question: What is the typical response of GFR maintenance mechanisms when mean arterial pressure falls below 80 mmHg?
options: GFR increases due to afferent arteriole constriction
GFR remains stable due to increased prostaglandin production
GFR declines steeply due to insufficient compensatory mechanisms
GFR is unaffected as long as efferent arteriolar tone is maintained
items.explanation: When mean arterial pressure falls below 80 mmHg, GFR declines steeply because the compensatory mechanisms are no longer sufficient.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.question: Which condition is a patient with bilateral renal artery stenosis particularly susceptible to when given ACE inhibitors or ARBs?
items.explanation: Patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis are particularly susceptible to a precipitous decline in GFR when given ACE inhibitors or ARBs due to reliance on efferent arteriolar vasoconstriction.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.question: What is the typical urine osmolality (>500 mosmol) indicative of in prerenal azotemia?
options: Intact tubules leading to concentrated urine
items.explanation: A urine osmolality greater than 500 mosmol in prerenal azotemia indicates intact tubules leading to concentrated urine.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.question: Which imaging study may be required to better define the presence or absence of obstructive uropathy when ultrasound is negative?
items.explanation: A furosemide renogram (MAG3 nuclear medicine study) may be required when ultrasound is negative to better define obstructive uropathy.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of acute renal failure cases are accounted for by urinary tract obstruction?
items.explanation: Urinary tract obstruction accounts for less than 5% of acute renal failure cases.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical urinalysis finding in allergic interstitial nephritis?
items.explanation: The classic sediment finding in allergic interstitial nephritis is a predominance of urinary eosinophils (>10%).
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What condition often accompanies atheroembolic renal failure?
items.explanation: Atheroembolic renal failure often accompanies livedo reticularis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.question: Which of the following findings is highly specific but very insensitive for glomerulonephritis?
items.explanation: RBC casts are highly specific but very insensitive for glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.question: What is the minimum urine output in a day that defines oliguria?
items.explanation: Oliguria refers to a 24-h urine output <400 mL.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.question: Which condition is associated with less severe disturbances of potassium and hydrogen balance compared to oliguric patients?
options: Anuria due to renal vein thrombosis
items.explanation: With nonoliguric ATN, disturbances of potassium and hydrogen balance are less severe than in oliguric patients.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.question: Which condition is most likely to be missed by dipstick screening for proteinuria?
items.explanation: Dipstick screening primarily detects albumin and may miss proteinuria not predominantly due to albumin, such as Bence-Jones proteins.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.question: What does a dipstick measurement of urine pH >7.0 potentially cause?
items.explanation: Dipstick measurement can give false-positive results at pH >7.0.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.question: What is the approximate correlation between a protein/creatinine ratio of 3.0 and daily proteinuria?
items.explanation: A protein/creatinine ratio of 3.0 correlates to approximately 3.0 g of proteinuria per day.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.question: Which mechanism involves the fusion of glomerular epithelial cell foot processes?
items.explanation: Minimal change disease causes fusion of glomerular epithelial cell foot processes, leading to selective albumin loss.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of isolated hematuria?
items.explanation: Hypercholesterolemia is related to nephrotic syndrome, not isolated hematuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.question: What condition can cause a false-positive dipstick for hematuria?
items.explanation: Myoglobinuria, often seen in rhabdomyolysis, can cause a false-positive dipstick for hematuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.question: What is the typical urinary finding in acute cystitis or urethritis in women?
items.explanation: Acute cystitis or urethritis can cause gross hematuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for unexplained isolated hematuria?
items.explanation: Hypercalcuria and hyperuricosuria are risk factors for unexplained isolated hematuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of solute diuresis leading to volume depletion and serum hypertonicity?
options: Excessive filtration of poorly reabsorbed glucose
items.explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes mellitus with glucosuria is the most common cause of a solute diuresis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.question: What condition can result from excessive filtration of poorly reabsorbed solutes like glucose or mannitol?
items.explanation: Excessive filtration of poorly reabsorbed solutes can lead to enhanced excretion in the urine, resulting in a solute diuresis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.question: Which condition is characterized by urine output >3 L/d and urine osmolality <250 mosmol/L?
items.explanation: This condition indicates a water diuresis, which can arise from polydipsia or inadequate secretion of AVP.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.statement: Disturbances in urine volume can include oliguria, anuria, and polyuria.
items.explanation: These are common disturbances in urine volume associated with renal syndromes.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.statement: The presence of hypertension is unrelated to nephrologic syndromes.
items.explanation: Hypertension can be a component of nephrologic syndromes.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.statement: Urea clearance is a reliable marker for estimating GFR because it is not reabsorbed by the tubules.
items.explanation: Urea clearance may underestimate GFR due to its reabsorption by the tubule.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.statement: The slope of the line representing the relationship between $1/P_{cr}$ and time remains constant for an individual unless there is a superimposed acute process.
items.explanation: In chronic progressive renal disease, this linear relationship's slope stays constant unless affected by an acute condition.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.statement: The CKD-EPI equation includes a coefficient to adjust eGFR based on self-reported black race.
items.explanation: The CKD-EPI equation uses a coefficient of 1.159 for self-reported black race.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.statement: Cystatin C levels are unaffected by the patient's sex and presence of diabetes mellitus.
items.explanation: Cystatin C levels are influenced by the patient's sex and the presence of diabetes mellitus.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 2)

items.statement: Prerenal failure is characterized by a fractional excretion of sodium (FE_Na) typically greater than 1%.
items.explanation: In prerenal failure, FE_Na is typically less than 1%, indicating avid sodium retention.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.statement: Patients with prerenal azotemia often have a urine Na concentration greater than 20 mmol/L.
items.explanation: In prerenal azotemia, the urine Na concentration is typically less than 20 mmol/L due to avid sodium retention.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.statement: Complete obstructive acute renal failure requires obstruction at the urethra or bladder outlet, bilateral ureteral obstruction, or unilateral obstruction in a patient with a single functioning kidney.
items.explanation: These conditions are necessary for complete obstructive acute renal failure due to the capability of a single kidney to provide adequate clearance.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Urinary eosinophils have diagnostic utility in renal disease.
items.explanation: Measurement of urine eosinophils has no diagnostic utility in renal disease as they can be increased in several other causes of AKI.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.statement: A finding of RBC casts in the urine is an indication for early renal biopsy.
items.explanation: RBC casts indicate glomerular injury and are important for diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.statement: Anuria can be caused by nonoliguric acute tubular necrosis.
items.explanation: Nonoliguric ATN is characterized by urine output >400 mL/d, not anuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.statement: A dipstick examination can accurately quantify total urine protein concentration.
items.explanation: Dipstick examinations primarily detect albumin and cannot accurately measure total urine protein concentration.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.statement: A protein/creatinine ratio can provide a rough estimate of daily protein excretion.
items.explanation: The protein/creatinine ratio in spot urine samples provides an approximate measure of daily proteinuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.statement: Hypertension is common in primary kidney diseases resulting in the nephrotic syndrome.
items.explanation: Hypertension is uncommon in primary kidney diseases causing nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.statement: Isolated pyuria without hematuria or casts is common.
items.explanation: Isolated pyuria is unusual as it typically accompanies hematuria.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.statement: A urine osmolality >300 mosmol/L with urine volume >3 L/d indicates a water diuresis.
items.explanation: This scenario indicates a solute diuresis, not a water diuresis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.statement: Primary polydipsia can result from psychiatric disorders or medications.
items.explanation: Primary polydipsia can indeed result from habit, psychiatric disorders, neurologic lesions, or medications.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POLYURIA

items.assertion: The specific combination of renal findings helps identify major nephrologic syndromes.
items.reason: These combinations reflect the underlying pathologic processes in the kidneys.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities

items.assertion: Direct measurement of GFR is feasible in most clinical settings.
items.reason: GFR can be directly measured using radioactive isotopes like inulin or iothalamate.
items.explanation: Direct measurement of GFR is not usually feasible in most clinical circumstances.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.assertion: Creatinine clearance can overestimate GFR due to secretion into the proximal tubule.
items.reason: In advanced CKD, creatinine is secreted through an organic cation pathway in the proximal tubule.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why creatinine clearance can overestimate GFR.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · ASSESSMENT OF GFR (part 1)

items.assertion: Prerenal failure can lead to acute tubular necrosis if renal hypoperfusion is prolonged.
items.reason: Prolonged renal hypoperfusion results in intrinsic renal damage, specifically acute tubular necrosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why prerenal failure can lead to acute tubular necrosis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.assertion: The presence of dark granular casts in urine sediment is indicative of prerenal azotemia.
items.reason: Dark granular casts are typically seen in acute tubular necrosis, not prerenal azotemia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dark granular casts indicate ATN; the reason is true as it correctly identifies the association of these casts with ATN.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · Azotemia | PRERENAL FAILURE

items.assertion: Ischemic ATN is observed most frequently in patients who have undergone major surgery.
items.reason: ATN often occurs due to renal hypoperfusion, which can be caused by severe hypovolemia or trauma associated with major surgery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ischemic ATN is observed in such patients.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Renal artery thrombosis typically leads to heavy proteinuria and hematuria.
items.reason: Renal vein thrombosis is associated with heavy proteinuria and hematuria.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because renal artery thrombosis may lead to mild proteinuria and hematuria, whereas the reason correctly describes renal vein thrombosis.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · POSTRENAL AZOTEMIA | INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA
items.reason: At least 400 mL of maximally concentrated urine must be produced to excrete the obligate daily osmolar load.
items.explanation: The requirement for a minimum urine output to handle the body's osmotic needs makes oliguria abnormal.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · INTRINSIC RENAL DISEASE (part 2) | OLIGURIA AND ANURIA

items.assertion: Dipstick screening is sufficient for detecting all types of proteinuria.
items.reason: Dipstick screening primarily detects albumin and may miss non-albumin proteins such as Bence-Jones proteins.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dipstick screening misses some types of proteinuria, while the reason correctly explains why.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.assertion: Proteinuria levels <30 mg/d do not increase risk for nephropathy or cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: Even low levels of albuminuria can be associated with increased risks for nephropathy and cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as even low albuminuria levels are linked to increased health risks, which the reason correctly states.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 1)

items.assertion: Hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs and protein excretion >500 mg/d is diagnostic of glomerulonephritis.
items.reason: RBC casts form as RBCs become trapped in a cylindrical mold of gelled Tamm-Horsfall protein.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS

items.assertion: A renal biopsy is needed for definitive diagnosis of IgA nephropathy.
items.reason: IgA nephropathy can lead to episodic gross hematuria.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the need for a renal biopsy.
items.reference: Azotemia and Urinary Abnormalities · PROTEINURIA (part 2) | HEMATURIA, PYURIA, AND CASTS
